title: From Sample Size to Effect-Size: Small Study Effect Investigation (SSEi)
Multivariate Methods and Small Sample Size: Combining with Small Effect Size
(Effect) Size Matters! Invited Commentary Related to
Lot-to-lot reagent verification: Effect of sample size and replicate measurement on linear regression approaches
How sample size can effect landslide size distribution
Animal pilot studies should not be used to estimate sample size if effect size and population variance are unknown
Issues in sample size determination when replicate measures are observed

items.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
               <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background and Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Researchers typically use Cohen’s guidelines of Pearson’s r = .10, .30, and .50, and Cohen’s d = 0.20, 0.50, and 0.80 to interpret observed effect sizes as small, medium, or large, respectively. However, these guidelines were not based on quantitative estimates and are only recommended if field-specific estimates are unknown. This study investigated the distribution of effect sizes in both individual differences research and group differences research in gerontology to provide estimates of effect sizes in the field.</jats:p>
               </jats:sec>
               <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Research Design and Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Effect sizes (Pearson’s r, Cohen’s d, and Hedges’ g) were extracted from meta-analyses published in 10 top-ranked gerontology journals. The 25th, 50th, and 75th percentile ranks were calculated for Pearson’s r (individual differences) and Cohen’s d or Hedges’ g (group differences) values as indicators of small, medium, and large effects. A priori power analyses were conducted for sample size calculations given the observed effect size estimates.</jats:p>
               </jats:sec>
               <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Effect sizes of Pearson’s r = .12, .20, and .32 for individual differences research and Hedges’ g = 0.16, 0.38, and 0.76 for group differences research were interpreted as small, medium, and large effects in gerontology.</jats:p>
               </jats:sec>
               <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Discussion and Implications</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Cohen’s guidelines appear to overestimate effect sizes in gerontology. Researchers are encouraged to use Pearson’s r = .10, .20, and .30, and Cohen’s d or Hedges’ g = 0.15, 0.40, and 0.75 to interpret small, medium, and large effects in gerontology, and recruit larger samples.</jats:p>
               </jats:sec>

affiliation.name: Department of Human Development and Family Studies, Colorado State University, Fort Collins

title: Effect Size Guidelines, Sample Size Calculations, and Statistical Power in Gerontology
